subject: ap-government
title: Quantitative Analysis — Composition of Federal Spending, Fiscal Years 1962–2019
stimulus: Source: Tax Policy Center, 2020. Line graph showing Composition of Federal Spending for Fiscal Years 1962–2019, displaying trends in mandatory spending, discretionary spending, and net interest as percentages of the total federal budget.

parts.prompt: Identify the type of spending that comprised the greatest percentage of the federal budget in 2016.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Identifies mandatory spending as the type that comprised the greatest percentage of the federal budget in 2016.
parts.scoring_notes: Only mandatory spending earns the point.

parts.prompt: Describe the difference in the trends in mandatory versus discretionary spending as illustrated in the graph.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Describes the contrasting trends. Accept: as a percentage of total federal spending, mandatory spending increased while discretionary spending decreased over the same time frame.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must describe both trends (mandatory increasing, discretionary decreasing) as a contrasting pattern. Describing only one trend does not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Draw a conclusion about Congress's budgetary choices based on trends in the data.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Draws a conclusion about congressional budgetary choices. Accept: mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress decreases discretionary spending; mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress raises taxes; mandatory spending increases over time, so Congress continues similar funding levels and increases the budget deficit.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must draw a conclusion (an inference about what Congress is choosing to do), not just describe the data. Accept any plausible conclusion about Congressional budgetary behavior supported by the trends.

parts.prompt: Explain how an interest group might respond to trends shown in the graph.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt: Explains a specific interest group response to the spending trends. Accept: as discretionary spending decreases, interest groups might be more motivated to fight for the survival of policies they support; interest groups might lobby for changes to mandatory spending programs that benefit their members; interest groups may lobby more aggressively for programs funded by discretionary spending since that funding is declining.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain a specific response connected to the spending trends — not just state that interest groups respond to spending. Accept any plausible and specific interest group response.
